OK. Here's my vote. I think in doing the JSON decode, that ONE statement in its own try block, we should trap anything that comes at up, and report it back as a parsing error in some form, either abusing an existing exception as I did, or introducing a new one. In the loop itself, we can remove the blanket exception, or more to my preference, keep it, message it, but uncomment the raise and let it continue to propagate. If we want to log more in those areas, I'm never opposed to that. I'm also in the "more information is better than less" camp.

WRT the callbacks, I think we should do the same. A callback should be trapping and reporting its own exceptions. If it gets to us, it's serious heartburn, but we should not let that take out the entire thread. On my Vera, there are 150 devices, and one device in particular occasionally gives an HA callback heartburn and knocks the whole thing out: it returns a string longer than 255 characters, and the HA callback chokes somewhere on it. That callback is making two bad choices, one of which is not trapping its own errors. I think we should blanket-catch that ONE statement (the invocation of the callback), log at error level the untrapped (now pyvera-trapped) exception, and continue on.

This gives those two cases VERY limited scope, with a reasonable expectation of good behavior on the part of the parser, and good self-management on the part of the callback. 

If this was my code, I would actually refactor the entire polling loop so that the get_changed call was in its own try small block (to catch whatever is passed on by that call that should not kill the thread--just enough to distinguish temporary errors from likely permanent ones), and the callback invocation was in its own one-statement try block, but everything else was naked, because really, there's nothing else that should be throwing elsewhere in that code, other than latent logic/coding errors or odd exceptions for which you would want a good, untrapped exception to be thrown (with its very accurate traceback associated).

That's my 2p. I'm open to whatever fits with your sensibilities. I am, of course, just trying to make the code harder to tip over.

I see, sorry for the confusion. Let me tell its story :bonfire:

Up until a week or so there was only a single argument, ``path`` stored in ``self.path``.

But then while working on the regex I noticed that as I was passing the full path (i.e. self.path) to the file, if the git checkout of the package being analyzed happened to be on ``/home/gil/source/tests/my.package``, then every single module analyzed would be marked as a test module.

For that I introduced a second argument on the init method (package_path) so that I could get only the relevant part of the filesystem path and thus avoid those problems.

If you look at the usage of ``_relative_path`` is only being used in ``_is_test_module`` method, nowhere else.

That's why I decided to keep it as a private variable and keep the other variable as ``path``.

Because future monkey patched some of the builtins for compatibility reasons. The code would look like this(except from `stacker/config/__init__.py`):

```
from __future__ import print_function
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import absolute_import
from future import standard_library
standard_library.install_aliases()
from builtins import str
import copy
import sys
```

flake8 would consider the last three imports should happen before `standard_library.install_aliases()`, wherein this case, since we are monkey patching, we cannot do what flake8 suggests.

We could alternatively disable E402 check in the code by writing `# noqa: E402` after the `install_aliases` call. Currently, I'm choosing the lazy way of disabling that check globally. I can add all these `noqa` lines if you want. :)

Here is something that might give an idea though, about when we create one of these:

* False is assigned to header.is_linked_to_previous
* header looks and sees whether it has a headerReference (or whatever that element is) of the right kind. Let's say it doesn't find one, which means it needs to create that header part.
* So first thing is it needs to create a new header part, then it needs to create a headerReference element to "link" to it with an rId.
* So it gets its part. That's probably a DocumentPart object, we won't care. It could be a glossary part can also have headers I don't know. All part and relationship management is done by another part. So we delegate the adding a header part to that part (definitely a DocumentPart in our case, we just won't hardwire that into the call). So the header code goes kind of like:

```python
part = self.part  # more on this later, but you can find it around in the code in places
# I'm not sure about this signature, but the gist of it will be
new_header_rId = part.add_header()
self._sectPr.add_header_reference(DEFAULT_HEADER, new_header_rId)
```

And that's all we do. We don't actually store a reference to the header, we only get that if we need it by referring to the XML and navigating the relationship etc. The XML stores the state and we don't retain a redundant copy of that state (because we don't want the job of keeping them synchronized).

Anyway, somewhere in all that a new `w:hdr` object gets created. I'm just not sure how yet. We should leave that decision until we get closer to it, probably until we're actually on the commit where we *have* to do it :) (that's the agile way :)

Python3's inspect module has slightly different behavior with ismethod in python2. inspect.ismethod returns true for unbound and bound methods. In Python3 there is no notion of unbound methods so ismethod returns only bound methods. Perhaps the pan-python methods were unbound causing it to fail with Python3?
"Unbound methods" are functions according to Python3, but they are methods in python2.
Thus, I needed the pred function to get the tests to work with Python2 and Python3.
TL;DR:
inspect.ismethod works with Python2 but not Python3
inspect.isfunction works with Python3 but not Python2

This is inaccurate. [The documentation](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.entity_mixins.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityCreateMixin) gives this brief description of `create_missing`:

> Populate required fields with random values. Required fields that already have a value are not populated. This method is not called by default.

Calling `.create(False)` is only necessary in Robottelo because Robottelo goes out of its way to change NailGun's default behaviour. [`robottelo/__init__.py`](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/robottelo/blob/master/robottelo/__init__.py) contains a call to [`configure_nailgun()`](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/robottelo/blob/master/robottelo/common/helpers.py#L282). What does that method do? Among other things, it enables automatic dependency creation:

```python
entity_mixins.CREATE_MISSING = True
```

> it might be overly optimistic to claim that the two master branches are always compatible with each other. Usually what happens is the server gets ahead of the Python driver, and then the Python driver catches up.

Yes, I agree.

It was nevertheless intentional on my part, for the time being to have it this way for a few reasons:

1. It is currently true.
2. In the context of the [DappHack]( http://www.dapphack.org/) event (DApp Hackathon and unConference about decentralised systems), and of our current users, I felt it was better to keep things as simple as possible.
3. I wanted to put a note about how there may be delays for the synchronization to occur, but was hesitant about the wording, and eventually felt it would be simpler to put no note for now, and to add one once it applies. In other words, I thought that if this row of the matrix could be dynamic that would be better.

That being said, what do you think about putting a simple and clear note explaining in a concise way that although we do our best to keep them in sync, there may be occasional delays?

If the HTTP server API contract is defined before the implementation, it should be possible to keep them in sync though, and even to have the driver(s) waiting for the server implementation.

It is a bug that I didn't catch during my review. To fix the bug, the `:` character needs to be removed. I commented [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/1969#discussion_r85807991) saying this should be:

```
if [ -n "${LE_AUTO_SUDO+x}" ]; then
```
This is a [POSIX compliant](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/utilities/V3_chap02.html#tag_18_06_02) way to test if `LE_AUTO_SUDO` string is set to anything, including the empty string. The idea is `LE_AUTO_SUDO` is set to the empty string when nothing special (such as running `su` or `sudo`) needs to be done to invoke Certbot. You can see this is used in the `case` statement following this `if`.

 If busybox doesn't recognize this, we could alternatively come up with an arbitrary string to designate this case.
